name: Nan Madol Full
description: Nan Madol, located off the coast of Pohnpei in Micronesia, is an ancient city built on artificial islets. The 'Nan Madol Full' concept encompasses various alternative theories extending beyond the mainstream archaeological understanding of the site. Mainstream archaeology attributes its construction to the Saudeleur Dynasty (c. 1180-1628), using readily available materials like basalt columns and coral fill. Proponents of alternative theories suggest a far older origin, possibly predating known Micronesian cultures, and often attribute construction to advanced, lost civilizations or even extraterrestrial involvement. These theories cite the sheer scale of the project, the precision of the basalt columns, and the lack of definitive evidence fully explaining the construction techniques as evidence. Some connect Nan Madol to the mythical lost continent of Lemuria or Mu, suggesting it was a major power center. Mainstream archaeology refutes these claims, pointing to radiocarbon dating placing construction within the timeframe of the Saudeleur Dynasty and arguing that while the construction was undoubtedly a monumental undertaking, it is within the realm of human capabilities using available resources and manpower. The lack of metal tools or advanced technology does not necessarily indicate extraterrestrial involvement but rather ingenuity and resourcefulness. Furthermore, the oral traditions of Pohnpei, while rich in mythology, consistently attribute the city to human builders, albeit with supernatural assistance. The 'Nan Madol Full' concept, therefore, represents a spectrum of alternative interpretations surrounding the site's origins and purpose.
subtitle: Alternative theories surrounding Nan Madol's origins

claims.claim: Nan Madol was built by a lost civilization predating known Micronesian cultures.
claims.evidence: The scale and precision of the stonework, the lack of definitive explanation for construction methods, and perceived similarities to other megalithic sites around the world.
claims.criticism: Radiocarbon dating places construction within the timeframe of the Saudeleur Dynasty. The construction, while impressive, is explainable through human ingenuity and resourcefulness using available materials. 'Similarities' to other megalithic sites are often superficial and lack concrete evidence.

claims.claim: Extraterrestrial beings were involved in the construction of Nan Madol.
claims.evidence: The seemingly impossible feats of engineering, the lack of metal tools at the site, and the alignment of certain structures with celestial events.
claims.criticism: The absence of metal tools does not necessitate alien involvement; it simply reflects the technology available to the builders. Celestial alignments, if present, can be attributed to astronomical knowledge possessed by the Saudeleur Dynasty. The 'impossible' feats of engineering are achievable with sufficient manpower and organization.

claims.claim: Nan Madol is connected to the mythical lost continents of Lemuria or Mu.
claims.evidence: The site's location in the Pacific Ocean, perceived similarities to descriptions of Lemuria/Mu, and anecdotal accounts linking Nan Madol to these lost lands.
claims.criticism: The existence of Lemuria and Mu is purely speculative and lacks any scientific or archaeological evidence. Linking Nan Madol to these mythical continents is based on conjecture and lacks factual support. Oral traditions from Pohnpei do not support this connection.

keyFigures.name: David Hatcher Childress
keyFigures.role: Promoter of alternative theories regarding Nan Madol's origins.

keyFigures.name: Rosalind Hunter-Anderson
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist specializing in Micronesian prehistory, providing mainstream perspectives on Nan Madol.

mythologyConnections: Stories of powerful sorcerers controlling the stones

timeline.event: Saudeleur Dynasty rules Pohnpei and constructs Nan Madol.
European explorers discover and document Nan Madol, sparking interest and speculation.
Alternative theories regarding Nan Madol's origins gain popularity through books, documentaries, and online forums.

sources.author: Childress, David Hatcher
sources.work: Lost Cities of Ancient Lemuria & the Pacific
sources.title: Childress, David Hatcher

sources.author: Hanlon, David L.
sources.work: Upon a Stone Altar: A History of the Island of Pohnpei to 1890
sources.title: Hanlon, David L.
